title: What World History Is
body:
World history is the study of humanity across time and across borders. It looks at the rise of agriculture, cities, empires, religions, trade systems, revolutions, industrialisation, colonisation, and the globalised world that followed. The goal is not just to list events, but to understand the forces that connect them.

A world history approach matters because no civilisation developed in isolation. Ideas, goods, diseases, technologies, and people have always moved across regions. The Silk Roads, the Indian Ocean, the trans-Saharan routes, and later the Atlantic world all linked societies together in ways that shaped what each of them became.

World history also asks who gets remembered. Traditional narratives often centre empires that left the biggest records, while forgetting the conquered, enslaved, or colonised. A good world history tries to widen the frame, not just to include more places, but to tell a more accurate story.
keyPoints: World history studies humanity as an interconnected whole
Trade, migration, war, and disease connect civilisations
No major society developed in complete isolation
Historical narratives often reflect power more than truth
Perspective matters as much as chronology
The subject helps explain the modern world
furtherReading: 'Guns, Germs, and Steel' by Jared Diamond
'A People's History of the World' by Chris Harman
